answers.value:
Riverbend Youth STEM Initiative is a community-based, 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 2016 in Columbus, Ohio, with the primary purpose of expanding access to high-quality science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education for young people ages 10–18 from low-income, historically underrepresented, and first-generation college-going families.

WHO WE SERVE:
We primarily serve middle and high school students living in the Franklinton, Hilltop, and South Side neighborhoods of Columbus—areas where more than 70% of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch and where many schools lack up-to-date STEM labs, equipment, and enrichment opportunities. Roughly 78% of our participants identify as Black, Latino, or multi-racial, 9% as recent immigrants or refugees (Somali, Nepali, and Congolese communities in particular), and over 60% will be the first in their families to attend college. We also work closely with parents and caregivers, many of whom work multiple jobs or did not have the opportunity to pursue higher education themselves.

WHAT WE DO:
Our core programs are:
1. After-School STEM Labs (Grades 6–8):
   - We operate four days per week at two partner middle schools and one community center. Students participate in hands-on projects such as building simple robots with Arduino kits, designing and 3D-printing prosthetic hands, and conducting environmental water-quality testing in the Scioto River.
   - Each cohort runs for 12 weeks, culminating in a community “STEM Showcase Night” where students present their projects to families, teachers, and local partners. Many parents have shared that this is the first time they’ve seen their children present in front of a group with that level of confidence.

2. High School Tech Pathways (Grades 9–12):
   - This program combines weekly coding workshops (Python, web development, and basic data analytics) with career exploration. We bring in volunteer mentors from local companies such as Cardinal Health and Nationwide to run resume workshops, mock interviews, and “day-in-the-life” panels.
   - Students complete a capstone project that addresses a real community need. For example, last year one team built a simple web app to help neighbors locate free food pantries and community fridges in Columbus. Another group created a data visualization of bus route gaps affecting students’ ability to reach after-school programs.

3. Summer STEM Exploration Camps:
   - We host two 3-week summer camps focusing on engineering design, environmental science, and digital media. Camps include field trips to The Ohio State University engineering labs, the Center of Science and Industry (COSI), and local manufacturing facilities so students can see real-world applications of what they’re learning.
   - Breakfast, lunch, and transportation are provided at no cost to families, which is critical because many parents cannot take time off work or afford private camps.

4. Family Engagement & College Readiness:
   - We hold quarterly Family STEM Nights where parents and siblings can participate in simple experiments (like building circuits with household materials) and learn about high school course planning, financial aid, and college admissions.
   - We provide one-on-one advising for juniors and seniors on FAFSA completion, scholarship applications, and navigating first-generation college questions (e.g., what to expect living on campus, how to choose a major, how to balance work and school).

HOW WE OPERATE & OUR IMPACT:
Our staff includes two full-time program coordinators (both former science teachers), one part-time family liaison fluent in Spanish and Somali, and a network of approximately 35 recurring volunteers—mostly college students in STEM majors and professionals from local tech firms. All staff and volunteers undergo background checks and receive training on trauma-informed practices, cultural humility, and inclusive teaching strategies.

Since our founding, we have served over 650 unique students, with approximately 180 participating in programs in the last academic year alone. Among students who participate for at least one full year:
- 87% report increased confidence in math and science.
- 74% enroll in at least one advanced STEM course (e.g., Algebra II, Chemistry, AP Computer Science) compared to 41% of a matched comparison group at the same schools.
- 92% of seniors who were in our program for two or more years graduated high school on time, and 68% enrolled in a 2- or 4-year college or a technical training program.

WHY WE EXIST:
We were founded by a small group of educators, parents, and engineers who saw a stark gap between the thriving tech and research sectors in Columbus and the lack of accessible STEM pathways for youth in under-resourced neighborhoods. One of our co-founders, for example, grew up in Franklinton and didn’t meet an engineer in person until college; she often shares with our students how different her trajectory might have been if she had seen people who looked like her working in STEM earlier in life.

Our underlying belief is that talent is evenly distributed, but opportunity is not. By providing free, consistent, and culturally responsive STEM programming, we aim to:
- Help students see themselves as problem-solvers and creators, not just consumers of technology.
- Increase the number of young people from underrepresented backgrounds who pursue STEM-related education and careers.
- Strengthen community resilience by equipping youth with skills that are relevant to the local job market (coding, data literacy, critical thinking, collaboration).

HOW WE BENEFIT THE COMMUNITY:
Beyond individual student outcomes, our projects are intentionally tied to local community needs. For instance:
- Our environmental science projects include monitoring water quality at three public access points along the Scioto River. Students share their findings with neighborhood associations and post simple, bilingual (English/Spanish) infographics in community centers.
- Several of our student-led tech projects support local nonprofits, such as creating simple websites or data dashboards to help them track attendance, volunteer hours, or outreach outcomes.
- We partner with Columbus Metropolitan Library branches to co-host pop-up STEM activities, which has increased youth library card sign-ups and after-school library usage.

In short, Riverbend Youth STEM Initiative exists to open doors for young people who might otherwise never see STEM as “for them,” and to do so in a way that is relational, community-rooted, and focused on long-term support rather than one-off events.

answers.value:
In addition to financial and volunteer support, we are specifically seeking in-kind donations of:

1. Equipment and Supplies:
   - 10–12 refurbished laptops (capable of running basic coding environments and web browsers smoothly) for students who currently have no reliable device at home. Many of our participants try to complete coding assignments on their phones, which is extremely limiting.
   - Additional Arduino and Raspberry Pi kits, sensors, and basic robotics components so that we can run more small-group projects without students having to wait to share a single kit among 5–6 people.
   - A small 3D printer and filament to expand our existing engineering design projects. Right now we borrow time on a partner organization’s printer, which creates scheduling bottlenecks and means some students don’t get to see their designs fully realized.

2. Space and Transportation Support:
   - Access to a larger multi-purpose room or lab space (even on a part-time/occasional basis) for our STEM Showcase Nights and family workshops. Our current community center room becomes overcrowded with more than 40 attendees, and we are now regularly hosting 60–80 family members at events.
   - Support or partnerships around transportation (e.g., bus passes, subsidized shuttle services, or coordination with existing youth transportation programs). Transportation is consistently one of the top barriers families mention when deciding whether their child can participate regularly.

3. Professional Services:
   - Pro bono support with impact evaluation and data analysis. While we collect attendance and basic outcome data, we would greatly benefit from guidance on designing more rigorous evaluation tools and translating our data into clear, funder-ready reports and visualizations.
   - Occasional pro bono legal or accounting consultation, especially around multi-year grant agreements and compliance as we grow. We want to ensure our policies keep pace with our expansion.

4. Strategic Partnerships:
   - Connections to local employers in technology, engineering, and advanced manufacturing who are interested in hosting site visits, offering job shadow days, or providing mentors for our high school students.
   - Partnerships with local colleges and universities to create clearer, more supportive pathways from our programs into bridge programs, dual-credit opportunities, and scholarships for our students.

We are open to creative forms of support that help us deepen and stabilize our programming—especially anything that improves student access (transportation, meals, technology) or strengthens the long-term sustainability of our organization (capacity-building, evaluation, and strategic planning support).
